does: This paper introduces PAIR, an automated algorithm that generates semantic jailbreaks against large language models using only black-box access. The method uses an attacker LLM to iteratively query and refine adversarial prompts targeting a victim LLM, inspired by social engineering tactics. Through automatic refinement loops without human intervention, PAIR achieves high jailbreaking success rates across both open-source models like Vicuna and closed-source systems including GPT-3.5 and GPT-4, often requiring fewer than twenty queries to produce effective jailbreaks—orders of magnitude more efficient than prior approaches.
connects: This work extends the emerging research on adversarial attacks against LLM safety mechanisms, building on earlier efforts to identify and exploit alignment vulnerabilities. It connects to prior work on prompt-based attacks, manual jailbreaking techniques, and adversarial examples in machine learning more broadly. The paper responds to the growing recognition that LLM alignment remains fragile despite safety training, and complements concurrent research on understanding failure modes of RLHF and other alignment techniques. By automating what was previously a labor-intensive manual process, PAIR advances the methodological toolkit for systematic adversarial evaluation of language models.
enables: This work enables more efficient discovery of safety vulnerabilities in deployed LLMs, supporting the development of more robust alignment methods and defenses. The high query efficiency and transferability of PAIR-generated jailbreaks could motivate research into prompt-level defenses and adversarial training techniques. The approach of using one LLM to attack another opens research directions in automated red-teaming frameworks and understanding the fundamental limits of behavioral alignment in language models. These findings inform the design of safer LLM architectures and evaluation protocols needed as models become more capable and widely deployed.
